name: AWS Access Key ID
message: Exposed AWS Access Key ID. Attackers can use this to scan your AWS resources.
fix: Remove the AWS Access Key from the code. Use AWS IAM roles or environment variables to inject the credentials at runtime.

name: Google / Gemini API Key
message: Exposed Google or Gemini API Key. Anyone can use your key to consume Gemini API quota, potentially incurring costs.
fix: Revoke this key immediately in the Google Cloud Console or Google AI Studio. Set up an environment variable (e.g. `GEMINI_API_KEY`) to load the key on your backend.

name: GitHub Personal Access Token
message: Exposed GitHub Personal Access Token. This can grant unauthorized access to your private repositories and GitHub account.
fix: Delete the token in your GitHub Developer Settings immediately. Re-generate a new token and store it securely in environment variables or GitHub Secrets.

name: Slack Webhook URL
message: Exposed Slack Incoming Webhook. Attackers can spam messages to your Slack channels.
fix: Deactivate this webhook URL in the Slack App Directory and generate a new one. Load the webhook URL via environment variables.

name: Stripe Secret API Key
message: Exposed Stripe API key. Attackers can execute charges, view customer data, and steal funds.
fix: Roll the secret key in the Stripe Dashboard immediately. Inject it into your application using environment variables.

name: Private SSH / Cryptographic Key
message: Exposed private SSH/cryptographic key. Attackers can gain access to servers, sign certificates, or decrypt traffic.
fix: Revoke the key immediately and generate a new key pair. Never commit private keys to version control. Add private key files to your `.gitignore`.

name: Generic Password in URL
message: Hardcoded database or service credentials in connection string URL.
fix: Use environment variables to store connection credentials. Replace the hardcoded username and password in the URL with `${DB_USER}:${DB_PASSWORD}`.
